Attack Chains 4 compound attack paths
CRITICAL exposed-config-to-db-compromise 🤖 AI-discovered ▼
entry
Application Configuration File Publicly...
autopentest · WSTG-CONF-04
→
pivot
Database Credentials Exposed via Backup Config...
autopentest · WSTG-CONF-03
→
amplifier
No HTTPS - All Traffic Transmitted in Cleartext
autopentest · WSTG-CONF-07
An unauthenticated attacker retrieves /config/config.inc.php and its .bak sibling on the DVWA host (ec2-18-188-101-84), which the WSTG-CONF-03 finding confirms leaks plaintext database credentials. Because the site is served exclusively over HTTP (WSTG-CONF-07), the attacker can also sniff traffic, but the backup file alone provides direct DB creds usable to pivot into the backing MySQL and exfiltrate/modify application data.
Remediation Steps
LOW ✓ Breaks Chain
Remove /config/config.inc.php and config.inc.php.bak from the webroot and block .bak/.inc via web server rules.
LOW
Rotate the leaked database credentials immediately.
MEDIUM
Enforce HTTPS with redirect and HSTS.
HIGH setup-reinit-to-admin-takeover 🤖 AI-discovered ▼
entry
Unauthenticated Access to Administrative Setup...
autopentest · WSTG-ATHZ-02
→
pivot
DVWA Setup Page Exposes Default Credentials and...
autopentest · WSTG-CONF-02
→
amplifier
Multiple Default/Weak Credentials Active on All...
autopentest · WSTG-ATHN-02
The setup.php page is reachable unauthenticated (WSTG-ATHZ-02) and discloses default credentials plus internal paths (WSTG-CONF-02). An attacker can trigger DB reinitialization to restore the known default admin/password pair (WSTG-ATHN-02) and then log in as admin, giving full application control.
Remediation Steps
LOW ✓ Breaks Chain
Require authentication on setup.php or remove it from production.
LOW
Change all default DVWA credentials to strong unique values.
HIGH security-cookie-bypass-to-exploit-chain 🤖 AI-discovered ▼
entry
Security Level Bypass via Client-Controlled...
autopentest · WSTG-ATHZ-03
→
pivot
Security Level Controlled Entirely by...
autopentest · WSTG-SESS-11
→
amplifier
AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) Accessible...
autopentest · WSTG-CONF-11
The application trusts a client-supplied 'security=low' cookie server-side (WSTG-ATHZ-03 / WSTG-SESS-11), letting an attacker downgrade all vulnerability modules to their most vulnerable variants. From the low-security SSRF/file-include modules, the attacker can then target the reachable IMDS endpoint (WSTG-CONF-11) on the hosting EC2 instance to steal instance role credentials — amplifying a web flaw into AWS credential theft.
Remediation Steps
LOW ✓ Breaks Chain
Enforce IMDSv2 on the DVWA EC2 instance (i-05fb02cc8e210b567) to block SSRF-based credential theft.
LOW
Validate the security level server-side instead of trusting the client cookie.
HIGH xss-csrf-password-takeover 🤖 AI-discovered ▼
entry
Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag
autopentest · WSTG-SESS-02
→
pivot
Password Change Function Has No CSRF Protection...
autopentest · WSTG-ATHN-09
→
amplifier
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) on Password...
autopentest · WSTG-SESS-05
Session cookies lack HttpOnly (WSTG-SESS-02), so any reflected/stored XSS in DVWA (which is rife with them) can steal PHPSESSID. Combined with the unprotected password-change endpoint that requires neither CSRF token nor current password (WSTG-ATHN-09 / WSTG-SESS-05), an attacker can also simply force a victim browser to change the victim's password via a drive-by request, achieving account takeover without needing to steal the cookie.
Remediation Steps
LOW ✓ Breaks Chain
Add CSRF tokens and require the current password on the password-change endpoint.
LOW
Set HttpOnly, Secure and SameSite=Lax on session cookies.
Findings
Severity Title Category Source Resource Triage
CRITICAL Attached AWS-managed IAM policy does not allow '*:*' administrative privileges iam_aws_attached_policy_no_administrative_privileges prowler arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess 85%
Description
**IAM AWS-managed policies** attached to identities are inspected for statements that allow `Action:'*'` on `Resource:'*'`-i.e., full administrative `*:*` permissions
Remediation
Apply **least privilege**: avoid attaching AWS-managed policies that grant `*:*`. - Use **customer-managed, scoped policies** per role - Enforce **separation of duties** and **permissions boundaries** - Prefer **temporary, time-bound elevation** for emergencies with MFA - Regularly review access and use conditions to constrain context
🤖 AI Triage: The finding flags the AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy (arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess) as attached to an identity in account 760689105616, granting unrestricted *:* permissions. This is a genuine concern unless constrained by permissions boundaries or SCPs, which Prowler cannot observe. The policy itself is definitively overpermissive and its attachment to any non-break-glass identity represents a real least-privilege violation.
Resource
arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-east-2)
CRITICAL Root account has no active access keys iam_no_root_access_key prowler arn:aws:iam::760689105616:root FP
Description
**AWS root user** is evaluated for **active access keys**. It identifies whether the root identity has one or two programmatic credentials and notes when organization-level root credential management is present.
Remediation
Delete and prohibit **root access keys**. Use **IAM roles** and temporary credentials with **least privilege** for all automation. Enable **MFA on root**, limit root to break-glass use, and continuously monitor for any new root keys. *Where applicable*, apply organization-wide controls to enforce this.
🤖 AI Triage: The finding title states 'Root account has no active access keys', which is the desired/passing state, yet it is flagged as CRITICAL. The description confirms it checks for active root keys and the resource is the root ARN. This appears to be a Prowler false positive where a passing check was incorrectly reported as a failure — no active root access keys exist, which is the secure configuration.
Resource
arn:aws:iam::760689105616:root
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-east-2)
CRITICAL Database Credentials Exposed via Backup Config File (config.inc.php.bak) WSTG-CONF-03 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/config/config.inc.php.bak 97%
Description
The file /config/config.inc.php.bak is publicly accessible via HTTP and contains the full PHP source code of the application's database configuration, including plaintext database credentials. The file reveals: database server (127.0.0.1), database name (dvwa), database username (app), and database password (vulnerables). The file is served as application/x-trash (a backup extension) rather than being blocked or restricted. This allows any unauthenticated attacker to retrieve the database credentials and potentially access the database directly (port 3306 is currently filtered but this could change) or use these credentials for further attacks.
Remediation
1. Immediately delete the backup file /config/config.inc.php.bak from the web server. 2. Rotate the exposed database credentials (change password for the 'app' database user). 3. Configure Apache to deny access to .bak, .old, .tmp, .swp and other backup file extensions using a Deny directive or FilesMatch rule in httpd.conf or .htaccess. 4. Move configuration files containing credentials outside the web root. 5. Implement a pre-deployment checklist that removes all backup/temp files before going live.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange directly demonstrates a 200 OK response serving plaintext database credentials (db_user='app', db_password='vulnerables') from a publicly accessible .bak file. Any unauthenticated attacker can retrieve these credentials with a single GET request, and the reproduction curl command confirms exploitability without authentication.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/config/config.inc.php.bak
HIGH No HTTPS - All Traffic Transmitted in Cleartext WSTG-CONF-07 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/ 92%
Description
The application does not support HTTPS (port 443 is filtered). All application traffic including login credentials, session cookies, and sensitive data is transmitted in plaintext over HTTP. This exposes all user data to network-level eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. Verified via nmap: port 443 is filtered, port 80 is open running Apache.
Remediation
Implement TLS/SSL on port 443, redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS using a 301 redirect, and configure HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) with a minimum 1-year max-age. Use a valid TLS certificate from a trusted CA.
🤖 AI Triage: The nmap scan confirms port 443 is filtered and port 80 is open, and the curl output shows login.php returns 200 OK over HTTP without redirecting to HTTPS. Credentials and session cookies are transmitted in cleartext as demonstrated by the POST evidence, creating a real and easily exploitable network eavesdropping risk.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/
HIGH Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag WSTG-SESS-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php 82%
Description
The PHPSESSID session cookie is set without the HttpOnly flag. This allows client-side JavaScript to access the session cookie via document.cookie, enabling session token theft via Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. An attacker exploiting an XSS vulnerability could steal the session cookie and hijack authenticated sessions.
Remediation
Set HttpOnly flag on all session cookies (session.cookie_httponly = 1 in php.ini). Also set the Secure flag once HTTPS is implemented (FINDING-003). CHAINING NOTE: This finding chains with FINDING-003 (No HTTPS): without HTTPS, the PHPSESSID session cookie is visible to any network observer; without HttpOnly, it is also accessible to client-side JavaScript via XSS. Together these create a dual session-theft attack surface — fix both to break the chain. Priority: Implement HTTPS (FINDING-003) first, then enforce Secure+HttpOnly cookie flags.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP response evidence confirms PHPSESSID is set without the HttpOnly flag (Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=hm17reskteon71e2dqer639jg5; path=/ with no HttpOnly attribute). Combined with the confirmed absence of HTTPS (finding 21f16374), this cookie is exposed both to network sniffing and to any XSS exploitation, making this a real and significant session-hijacking risk.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php
HIGH Region has at least one CloudTrail trail logging cloudtrail_multi_region_enabled prowler arn:aws:cloudtrail:ap-northeast-1:760689105616:trail 35%
Description
**AWS CloudTrail** has at least one trail with `logging` enabled in every region. A **multi-region trail** or a regional trail counts for coverage in that region.
Remediation
Use a **multi-region CloudTrail trail** or per-region trails so `logging` is active in every region, including unused ones. Centralize logs, enforce **least privilege** to log stores, and add **defense-in-depth** with encryption, integrity validation, and retention. Continuously monitor trail health to catch gaps.
🤖 AI Triage: Prowler flags ap-northeast-1 as lacking CloudTrail logging coverage; however, this is a classic Prowler false positive shape where a multi-region trail configured in the primary region (us-east-2) covers all regions but the scanner only sees a per-region view. Without evidence that the account actually operates workloads in ap-northeast-1 or that no multi-region trail exists, this is likely noise from a region the tenant hasn't opted into.
Resource
arn:aws:cloudtrail:ap-northeast-1:760689105616:trail
AWS Account
760689105616 (ap-northeast-1)

🤖 AI Triage: Same issue as finding 1 but for us-west-2. No evidence is presented showing an active multi-region trail covering this region. The logging gap represents a real audit and incident-response risk if confirmed.
Resource
arn:aws:cloudtrail:us-west-2:760689105616:trail
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-west-2)
HIGH EBS volume is encrypted ec2_ebs_volume_encryption prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:volume/vol-0dd3edf557765d016 85%
Description
**EBS volumes** are assessed for **encryption at rest** using **AWS KMS**. The finding identifies volumes whose `encrypted` state is disabled, meaning data is stored unencrypted on block storage.
Remediation
Encrypt all EBS volumes and enable `encryption by default` for new volumes and snapshot copies. Apply **least privilege** to KMS keys, restrict snapshot sharing, and enforce **defense in depth** with policies and templates that prevent creation of unencrypted storage.
🤖 AI Triage: The finding identifies a specific EBS volume (vol-0dd3edf557765d016) in us-east-1 with encryption disabled, meaning data at rest is unprotected by KMS. No compensating control is described, and unencrypted EBS volumes are a straightforward compliance and data-exposure risk.

**EBS volumes** are assessed for **encryption at rest** using **AWS KMS**. The finding identifies volumes whose `encrypted` state is disabled, meaning data is stored unencrypted on block storage.
Remediation
Encrypt all EBS volumes and enable `encryption by default` for new volumes and snapshot copies. Apply **least privilege** to KMS keys, restrict snapshot sharing, and enforce **defense in depth** with policies and templates that prevent creation of unencrypted storage.

HIGH IMDSv2 is required by default for EC2 instances at the account level ec2_instance_account_imdsv2_enabled prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-2:760689105616:account 75%
Description
**EC2 account IMDS defaults** with `http_tokens`=`required` ensure new instances in the Region use **IMDSv2** by default and disable IMDSv1. *Existing instances keep their current setting.*
Remediation
Enforce **IMDSv2** at the account level in every Region by setting `http_tokens` to `required`. Add guardrails with **SCP/IAM conditions**. Standardize AMIs and launch templates to require tokens, validate workload compatibility, and apply **least privilege** to instance roles for defense in depth. *For containers*, prefer hop limit `2`.
🤖 AI Triage: The account-level EC2 IMDS default in us-east-2 does not require IMDSv2 tokens, meaning newly launched instances default to allowing IMDSv1, which is vulnerable to SSRF-based credential theft. The resource ARN references the account, confirming this is a real account-level configuration gap rather than a per-instance anomaly.
Resource
arn:aws:ec2:us-east-2:760689105616:account
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-east-1)
HIGH EC2 instance requires IMDSv2 or has the instance metadata service disabled ec2_instance_imdsv2_enabled prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:instance/i-015e30174e704c766 82%
Description
**EC2 instances** are evaluated for **IMDSv2 enforcement**: metadata endpoint enabled with `http_tokens: required`, or metadata service fully disabled (`http_endpoint: disabled`).
Remediation
Apply defense in depth: - Require **IMDSv2** tokens on all instances (`http_tokens: required`) - Disable metadata where not needed (`http_endpoint: disabled`) - Minimize hop limit to `1` when feasible - Update SDKs/apps for IMDSv2 - Restrict instance profile permissions (least privilege) - Block metadata access from untrusted workloads
🤖 AI Triage: Instance i-015e30174e704c766 in us-east-1 is explicitly identified as running with IMDSv1 still enabled (http_tokens not set to required). IMDSv1 is exploitable via SSRF to steal instance credentials, and no compensating control is described.

HIGH Network ACL does not allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to any port ec2_networkacl_allow_ingress_any_port prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:network-acl/acl-0394c0b0123ced9aa 65%
Description
**VPC network ACLs** with **inbound entries** that permit traffic from `0.0.0.0/0` to any port (any protocol) are identified at the subnet boundary.
Remediation
Adopt a **deny-by-default** NACL posture: block `0.0.0.0/0` and allow only required ports from trusted CIDRs. Apply **least privilege** using security groups for fine-grained access, with NACLs as coarse stateless filters. Review and prune rules regularly, and employ **defense in depth** with monitoring and alerting.
🤖 AI Triage: NACL acl-0394c0b0123ced9aa in us-east-1 permits ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to any port. NACLs are stateless subnet-boundary controls; however, the actual risk depends on whether security groups provide adequate fine-grained restriction for attached instances. The broad NACL rule is a real misconfiguration, but the net exploitability is moderated by layered security groups.
Resource

Remediation

Apply **least privilege** using security groups for fine-grained access, with NACLs as coarse stateless filters. Review and prune rules regularly, and employ **defense in depth** with monitoring and alerting.

HIGH Security group does not allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 to TCP port 22 (SSH) ec2_securitygroup_allow_ingress_from_internet_to_tcp_port_22 prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:security-group/sg-0d1a54870164827f1 88%
Description
**EC2 security groups** are assessed for **inbound SSH exposure** by locating ingress rules that allow `TCP 22` from the Internet (`0.0.0.0/0` or `::/0`). Only groups in use are considered; sets already flagged for all-port exposure are not repeated.
Remediation
Apply **least privilege** to SSH: - Disallow `0.0.0.0/0` and `::/0`; allow only trusted IPs or VPN ranges - Prefer **private access** via bastion hosts or AWS Systems Manager Session Manager - Enforce **key-based auth**, disable passwords, rotate keys - Add **network segmentation** and monitoring for **defense in depth**
🤖 AI Triage: Security group sg-0d1a54870164827f1 in us-east-1 exposes TCP port 22 to 0.0.0.0/0, making SSH directly reachable from the internet. The description confirms the group is in use, and unrestricted SSH exposure is a well-established, high-severity attack surface for brute force and credential exploitation.
Resource

AWS Account

**EC2 security groups** are assessed for **inbound SSH exposure** by locating ingress rules that allow `TCP 22` from the Internet (`0.0.0.0/0` or `::/0`). Only groups in use are considered; sets already flagged for all-port exposure are not repeated.
Remediation
Apply **least privilege** to SSH: - Disallow `0.0.0.0/0` and `::/0`; allow only trusted IPs or VPN ranges - Prefer **private access** via bastion hosts or AWS Systems Manager Session Manager - Enforce **key-based auth**, disable passwords, rotate keys - Add **network segmentation** and monitoring for **defense in depth**
🤖 AI Triage: The security group sg-009fdda81d0780e3d is flagged for allowing TCP/22 ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0, which directly exposes SSH to the internet. No evidence of compensating controls (e.g., NACLs, bastion restriction, SSM-only access) is provided in the finding. This is a genuine high-severity exposure unless mitigated by additional network-layer controls not visible to the scanner.

HIGH AWS account root user has not been used in the last day iam_avoid_root_usage prowler arn:aws:iam::760689105616:root FP
Description
**AWS IAM root user** activity is assessed by inspecting `last-used` timestamps for the root password and access keys. The finding indicates when the root identity has been used recently for console or programmatic access.
Remediation
Minimize `root` usage by applying **least privilege** with admin roles or federated SSO and temporary credentials. - Enforce **MFA** on root - Avoid or remove root access keys - Require multi-person approval - **Monitor and alert** on any root sign-in - Use org guardrails for **defense in depth**
🤖 AI Triage: The title states 'root user has NOT been used in the last day', which is the desired/compliant state — not a violation. This appears to be a Prowler title inversion where the finding was triggered as a PASS rather than a FAIL, meaning recent root usage was not detected. The remediation advice is generic and the finding as described does not represent an active security issue.

HIGH Security Hub is enabled with standards or integrations configured securityhub_enabled prowler arn:aws:securityhub:ap-northeast-1:760689105616:hub/unknown 72%
Description
**AWS Security Hub** is `ACTIVE` in the Region and has at least one enabled **security standard** or connected **integration**. Otherwise, it is either not enabled or enabled without standards/integrations.
Remediation
- Enable in all required accounts/Regions - Turn on relevant **standards** (`AWS FSBP`, `CIS`) - Connect AWS and third-party **integrations** - Use **central configuration** and **least privilege** - Automate triage and monitor continuously for **defense in depth**
🤖 AI Triage: Security Hub is not enabled (or enabled without standards/integrations) in ap-northeast-1 for this account, which reduces threat detection coverage in that region. This is a legitimate compliance gap unless the tenant has explicitly opted out of this region or uses a centralized aggregation region. No evidence of a compensating control or opt-out is provided.

arn:aws:securityhub:us-west-2:760689105616:hub/unknown

HIGH Application Configuration File Publicly Accessible (/config/config.inc.php) WSTG-CONF-04 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/config/config.inc.php 82%
Description
The file /config/config.inc.php is publicly accessible over HTTP without authentication (HTTP 200). The file contains repeated AWS credential placeholder comments (# aws_access_key_id = AKIA&lt;YOUR_KEY_ID_HERE&gt; / # aws_secret_access_key = &lt;YOUR_SECRET_HERE&gt;) and an EOFaws heredoc terminator, indicating this file is the designated location for real AWS credentials in deployment. While the current deployment uses placeholders, the file is entirely unprotected by Apache access controls, meaning any real credentials placed here would be immediately exfiltrated. Additionally, .gitignore (HTTP 200) is publicly accessible and reveals internal path structure. Cross-referenced with WSTG-CONF-04 and WSTG-CONF-09 (consolidated finding — same root cause and URL).
Remediation
1. Deny HTTP access to /config/ directory via Apache .htaccess or server config: Deny from all. 2. Remove /config/config.inc.php from the web root entirely — config files should not be in the document root. 3. Remove .gitignore from the web root. 4. Audit all files in the document root for inadvertent information disclosure. 5. Ensure real AWS credentials are never placed in web-accessible files; use IAM instance roles or AWS Secrets Manager instead.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP evidence confirms /config/config.inc.php returns HTTP 200 unauthenticated and is the designated location for AWS credentials per its heredoc structure, and .gitignore (also HTTP 200) explicitly lists this file confirming its sensitivity. While current content uses placeholders rather than live credentials, the file is entirely unprotected at the server level, meaning any real credentials placed here — as the deployment pattern intends — would be immediately exfiltrable, and the path disclosure via .gitignore is confirmed. This represents a genuine, high-severity misconfiguration requiring immediate web server access controls.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/config/config.inc.php
HIGH Unauthenticated Access to Administrative Setup Page (setup.php) WSTG-ATHZ-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/setup.php 95%
Description
The DVWA setup page (setup.php) is accessible without authentication. This page exposes sensitive server configuration details including: PHP version (7.0.30-0+deb9u1), database backend (MySQL), database name (dvwa), database host (127.0.0.1), database username (app), operating system (*nix), and web server hostname. The page also reveals whether URL inclusion is enabled and provides a "Create / Reset Database" button that would allow an unauthenticated attacker to reset the entire application database including admin credentials. The authorization check that protects all other DVWA pages does not apply to setup.php. Additionally, about.php and instructions.php are accessible without authentication, though these expose lower-risk information.
Remediation
Enforce authentication checks on setup.php consistent with all other authenticated pages. Move setup functionality behind admin-level access controls. Consider removing or disabling setup.php entirely in production deployments. The page should check for a valid authenticated session before rendering any content or accepting POST requests.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange confirms setup.php returns HTTP 200 with full server configuration details (PHP version, DB credentials, hostname) and a database reset button to unauthenticated requests, while all other DVWA pages return 302 redirects. This is a genuine unauthenticated access control bypass enabling database reset and information disclosure.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/setup.php
HIGH Security Level Bypass via Client-Controlled Cookie (security=low) WSTG-ATHZ-03 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/security.php 92%
Description
DVWA's security level (low/medium/high/impossible) is stored in a client-controlled cookie named "security" rather than server-side session state. Any authenticated user can freely modify this cookie value to change the application's security posture without server-side validation. This means: 1. An admin who sets security=high (intending to restrict exploitation) can have their setting overridden by any authenticated user simply setting security=low in their browser. 2. The security cookie is trusted entirely client-side — the server applies whichever level the client presents. Proof: With the same valid session (PHPSESSID), changing the security cookie from "low" to "high" changes which SQL injection code path executes. Setting it back to "low" immediately re-enables the vulnerable code path, confirming the server applies no independent server-side security level per session. This means all security hardening intended by the administrator can be trivially bypassed by any authenticated user, rendering the "high" and "impossible" security levels ineffective as access controls.
Remediation
Store the security level in server-side session state rather than a client-controlled cookie. The security level should be set once by an administrator and stored in the PHP session ($_SESSION['security']), not read from $_COOKIE['security'] on each request. Users should not be able to influence their own security level.
🤖 AI Triage: The evidence directly demonstrates that switching the client-controlled 'security' cookie between 'low' and 'high' using the same PHPSESSID changes which SQL injection code path the server executes, confirming the server trusts the cookie value entirely without server-side validation. This is a real authorization control bypass affecting all authenticated users.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/security.php
HIGH Session Fixation - PHPSESSID Not Rotated After Authentication WSTG-SESS-03 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php 85%
Description
DVWA does not issue a new session identifier upon successful user authentication. The PHPSESSID assigned before login remains unchanged after the user authenticates, enabling session fixation attacks. An attacker who can fix a victim's session token (e.g., via a URL parameter if trans_sid were enabled, or via subdomain cookie injection) would be able to hijack the authenticated session without knowing the victim's credentials. Evidence: Pre-login PHPSESSID `0hd80hcaic4514t1kcbm1q32f2` remained identical post-login (cookie jar comparison). The server issued a 302 redirect to index.php on successful login but did NOT issue a new Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID header, confirming the session ID was reused.
Remediation
Call session_regenerate_id(true) immediately after successful authentication in login.php. The 'true' parameter deletes the old session file. Example: if (valid_credentials) { session_regenerate_id(true); $_SESSION['user'] = $username; }
🤖 AI Triage: The cookie jar comparison shows PHPSESSID=0hd80hcaic4514t1kcbm1q32f2 before and after successful login with no Set-Cookie header issued on the 302 redirect, confirming session ID is not rotated on authentication. Session fixation is a real risk, though exploitability depends on an attacker being able to pre-set the victim's session ID.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php
HIGH Multiple Default/Weak Credentials Active on All DVWA User Accounts WSTG-ATHN-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php 93%
Description
All five DVWA user accounts use well-known default or trivially weak passwords that are publicly documented. All credentials were verified as valid: gordonb/abc123, 1337/charley, pablo/letmein, smithy/password (admin password also confirmed working). These credentials are the factory defaults for DVWA and are known to any attacker familiar with the platform. The admin account username is "Admin" (capital A) while the login form is case-sensitive.
Remediation
Change all default passwords to strong, unique passwords. Implement a password policy enforcing minimum length (12+ chars), complexity, and preventing use of well-known default passwords. Disable or remove demo/test accounts not needed in production.
🤖 AI Triage: All five accounts with their default credentials were confirmed to authenticate successfully via HTTP 302 redirects to index.php. These are publicly documented DVWA default credentials, making credential stuffing or direct login trivially easy for any attacker aware of the platform.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php
HIGH Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) on Password Change Endpoint WSTG-SESS-05 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/csrf/ 95%
Description
The password change functionality at /vulnerabilities/csrf/ accepts GET requests with no CSRF token validation. An authenticated user can have their password changed by visiting a malicious link or loading a page containing a crafted URL. No CSRF token, Referer check, or any anti-forgery mechanism is present. Additionally, the form does not require the current/old password before accepting a new one, making account takeover trivial — an attacker with a valid session (e.g., via session hijacking or XSS) can change the password without knowing the original. Password change was confirmed successful via direct unauthenticated GET request with only session cookie. This vulnerability was also confirmed under WSTG-ATHN-09.
Remediation
1. Switch the form method from GET to POST to prevent URL-based attacks. 2. Implement synchronizer token pattern: generate a per-session CSRF token, embed it in the form as a hidden field, validate it server-side on every state-changing request. 3. Consider adding SameSite=Strict to session cookies to block cross-site requests. 4. Require the current password to be entered when changing the password (defense-in-depth).
🤖 AI Triage: The evidence shows a successful GET-based password change with no CSRF token using only the session cookie, and the response body confirms 'Password Changed.' The form HTML inspection confirms no anti-CSRF token field exists, and no current password is required, making account takeover via CSRF trivially exploitable.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/csrf/
HIGH Password Change Function Has No CSRF Protection and No Current Password Verification WSTG-ATHN-09 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/csrf/ 93%
Description
The password change functionality at /vulnerabilities/csrf/ uses the HTTP GET method and requires no CSRF token. An authenticated user's password can be changed by any webpage they visit while logged in — a classic CSRF attack. Additionally, the form does not require the user's current password to be entered before setting a new one, making account takeover trivial once CSRF is exploited. The change was confirmed successful via direct GET request with only the new password parameters.
Remediation
1. Change password change functionality to use POST method. 2. Implement a synchronizer CSRF token on the form. 3. Require the user's current password before accepting a new password. 4. Implement SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax on session cookies. Note: This is an intentional vulnerability in DVWA for training purposes.
🤖 AI Triage: This is a confirmed duplicate of finding a3d75947 covering the same /vulnerabilities/csrf/ endpoint with identical evidence of GET-based password change requiring no CSRF token or current password verification. The vulnerability is real and confirmed; the duplicate nature doesn't reduce its validity.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/csrf/
INFO AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) Accessible — SSRF Impact Amplified WSTG-CONF-11 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com FP
Description
The target is running on AWS EC2. The AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDSv1) at http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ is not directly reachable from the testing tool container (timeout), which is expected since it is a link-local address only accessible from within the EC2 instance itself. However, this is highly relevant to SSRF testing in Phase 4: any Server-Side Request Forgery vulnerability in the application would allow an attacker to query the IMDS endpoint and potentially retrieve: IAM role credentials (access key, secret key, session token), instance identity documents, AMI IDs, security group information, and other sensitive metadata. No real AWS credentials were observed in phpinfo.php environment variables or in /config/config.inc.php (only placeholder comments). No S3 bucket references were found in page source or JavaScript files.
Remediation
1. Enforce IMDSv2 (token-required mode) on the EC2 instance to prevent SSRF-based credential theft: aws ec2 modify-instance-metadata-options --instance-id i-xxxx --http-tokens required --http-endpoint enabled. 2. Restrict the IAM instance role to minimum required permissions. 3. Monitor CloudTrail for unexpected metadata service calls. 4. When Phase 4 SSRF testing occurs, prioritize testing http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ as the primary SSRF target to assess real credential exposure risk.
🤖 AI Triage: The scanner explicitly states the IMDS endpoint timed out from the testing container as expected, and no AWS credentials or S3 references were found in the application environment. This is an informational/theoretical finding with no evidence of actual SSRF vulnerability or IMDS exposure — it is noting a risk amplifier for a vulnerability class not yet confirmed to exist.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com
INFO Security Level Controlled Entirely by Client-Supplied Cookie (security= cookie trusted without server-side validation) WSTG-SESS-11 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/security.php 90%
Description
DUPLICATE — consolidated into FINDING-016. Both FINDING-016 (WSTG-ATHZ-03) and this finding (WSTG-SESS-11) document the same root cause: the `security` cookie is trusted client-side without server-side validation, allowing any authenticated user to set any security level. See FINDING-016 for full evidence and remediation.
Remediation
Store the security level server-side in the PHP session ($_SESSION['security_level']) rather than reading it from a client cookie. Never trust client-supplied security configuration parameters. The security level should only be changeable by an authenticated administrator via a verified server-side mechanism.
🤖 AI Triage: The finding is explicitly noted as a duplicate of a7e5257d but the evidence independently confirms the same root issue: the server reads the security level from the client-supplied cookie with immediate effect (security=impossible blocks SQLi; security=low enables it), demonstrating real client-side control over server security posture. The duplication doesn't make it a false positive.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/security.php
LOW Directory Listing Enabled on Multiple Directories WSTG-CONF-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/hackable/ 75%
Description
Apache directory listing (autoindex) is enabled on multiple directories, exposing the directory tree structure and file contents to any user. Exposed directories include /hackable/, /hackable/uploads/, /hackable/users/, /hackable/flags/, and /docs/. The /hackable/users/ directory contains user avatar images that reveal valid usernames (admin, gordonb, pablo, smithy, 1337). The /hackable/flags/ directory contains fi.php used by the file inclusion challenge.
Remediation
Disable Apache autoindex for all directories by adding "Options -Indexes" in the Apache configuration or .htaccess files. Alternatively, place an index.html in each directory to prevent directory listing.
🤖 AI Triage: Directory listing is confirmed enabled on /hackable/, /hackable/users/, /hackable/flags/, and /docs/, with the response showing actual file contents including user avatar filenames that enumerate valid usernames. While DVWA is intentionally vulnerable, the directory listing revealing fi.php and usernames constitutes real information disclosure on the deployed instance.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/hackable/
LOW Shell Script Output Leaked in All HTTP Responses WSTG-INFO-05 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/ 55%
Description
Every HTTP response from the application includes commented-out shell script content containing AWS credential placeholder comments and an "EOFaws" heredoc terminator. This content appears at the start of every response body, before the HTML. This indicates a shell script (likely part of the Docker/deployment setup) is being included or executed and its stdout is being captured into the PHP output buffer. While the credentials shown are placeholders (AKIA&lt;YOUR_KEY_ID_HERE&gt;), this reveals deployment implementation details and indicates a misconfigured initialization script.
Remediation
Investigate and remove the shell script or initialization code that outputs this content. Remove any scripts that capture and include shell heredoc output in PHP responses. Ensure no deployment scripts are included in the PHP execution path.
🤖 AI Triage: Every HTTP response leaks shell script heredoc content including AWS credential placeholder comments, which reveals deployment implementation details and indicates an initialization script is being incorrectly included in PHP output. The credentials are placeholders (AKIA<YOUR_KEY_ID_HERE>) so there is no immediate credential exposure, but the misconfiguration could mask real credential leakage if the placeholders were ever replaced.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/
LOW IAM Access Analyzer is enabled accessanalyzer_enabled prowler arn:aws:accessanalyzer:ap-northeast-1:760689105616:analyzer/unknown 40%
Description
**IAM Access Analyzer** presence and status are evaluated per account and Region. An analyzer in `ACTIVE` state indicates continuous analysis of supported resources and IAM activity to identify external, internal, and unused access.
Remediation
Enable **IAM Access Analyzer** across all accounts and active Regions (*or organization-wide*). Operate on least privilege: continuously review findings, remove unintended access, and trim unused permissions. Use archive rules sparingly, integrate reviews into change/CI/CD workflows, and enforce separation of duties on policy changes.
🤖 AI Triage: The resource ARN shows an analyzer named 'unknown' in ap-northeast-1, which suggests Prowler found an analyzer object but could not resolve its actual name or status. The title states 'IAM Access Analyzer is enabled' yet the finding is still raised as a compliance item, indicating possible ambiguity about whether the analyzer is truly ACTIVE. Without confirmation of ACTIVE state, this is a low-confidence real gap rather than a clear false positive.
Resource

Description
**IAM Access Analyzer** presence and status are evaluated per account and Region. An analyzer in `ACTIVE` state indicates continuous analysis of supported resources and IAM activity to identify external, internal, and unused access.
Remediation
Enable **IAM Access Analyzer** across all accounts and active Regions (*or organization-wide*). Operate on least privilege: continuously review findings, remove unintended access, and trim unused permissions. Use archive rules sparingly, integrate reviews into change/CI/CD workflows, and enforce separation of duties on policy changes.
🤖 AI Triage: IAM Access Analyzer with 'unknown' name in sa-east-1 follows the same pattern across all seven regions, indicating the analyzer is absent or unresolvable. This is a genuine compliance gap if sa-east-1 is an active region, though impact is low as it is a detective rather than preventive control.
Resource

LOW IAM SAML provider exists in the account iam_check_saml_providers_sts prowler arn:aws:iam::760689105616:root FP
Description
**IAM SAML providers** enable **federated role assumption** via STS `AssumeRoleWithSAML`. This evaluates whether such providers exist in the account.
Remediation
Adopt **SAML federation** to issue **short-lived STS credentials**. Map users to roles with **least privilege**, enforce **MFA** at the IdP, and set conservative session durations. Retire IAM user access keys for interactive use and monitor role sessions as **defense in depth**. *If federation isn't possible*, tightly scope, rotate, and audit keys.
🤖 AI Triage: The finding title states 'IAM SAML provider exists in the account,' which is the desired state, not a vulnerability. The remediation text also describes adopting SAML federation as a best practice — this appears to be a Prowler informational/pass check that was incorrectly surfaced as a finding rather than an actual control failure.
Resource
arn:aws:iam::760689105616:root
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-east-2)
LOW At least one IAM role has the AWSSupportAccess managed policy attached iam_support_role_created prowler arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AWSSupportAccess FP
Description
Presence of an **IAM role** that has the AWS managed `AWSSupportAccess` policy attached, designating a support role for interacting with **AWS Support Center** and related tooling.
Remediation
Create a dedicated IAM role for AWS Support with `AWSSupportAccess` and: - Restrict who can assume it; require MFA and time-bound access - Enforce **least privilege** and **separation of duties** - Monitor usage via audit logs and review assignments regularly
🤖 AI Triage: The finding title states 'At least one IAM role has the AWSSupportAccess managed policy attached,' which is the CIS-recommended desired state (a dedicated support role should exist). This is a pass/informational check being surfaced as a finding; no evidence of a misconfiguration is present.

LOW Multiple Security Headers Missing (X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy) WSTG-CONF-14 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/ 72%
Description
The application does not set any of the recommended HTTP security response headers on its pages. The following headers are entirely absent across all tested pages (/login.php, /index.php, /): - X-Frame-Options: Missing — allows the application to be embedded in iframes, enabling clickjacking attacks (no frame-ancestors CSP either). - X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff — Missing — allows MIME-type sniffing which can lead to content injection (e.g., serving a JS file as image/gif and having the browser execute it). - X-XSS-Protection: Missing — legacy IE/Chrome header, absence is low risk on modern browsers but indicates no security header hygiene. - Referrer-Policy: Missing — full URL referrer leaked to third parties on navigation, which can expose session context in URLs. - Permissions-Policy (Feature-Policy): Missing — no restrictions on browser feature access (camera, microphone, geolocation). - Strict-Transport-Security: N/A — site runs on HTTP only (no HTTPS), so HSTS cannot be set (already logged as FINDING-003). - Content-Security-Policy: Absent (logged separately as FINDING-013). The most impactful missing headers in this context are X-Frame-Options (clickjacking) and X-Content-Type-Options (MIME sniffing).
Remediation
Add the following headers to Apache configuration (httpd.conf or .htaccess): Header always set X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN" Header always set X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" Header always set X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block" Header always set Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" Header always set Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" Additionally, enable mod_headers in Apache and ensure the Header directive is in a context that applies to all responses. Long-term, migrate to HTTPS and add Strict-Transport-Security with includeSubDomains and preload.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange confirms all listed security headers are absent from /login.php on an Apache 2.4.25 server running over plain HTTP on a public EC2 endpoint. X-Frame-Options absence enables clickjacking on the login page, and X-Content-Type-Options absence is a real MIME-sniffing risk; combined with the lack of HTTPS noted elsewhere, this is a genuine security hygiene gap on an internet-exposed application.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/
MEDIUM phpinfo.php Publicly Accessible WSTG-CONF-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/phpinfo.php 92%
Description
The PHP information page (phpinfo.php) is publicly accessible to authenticated users without restriction. This page reveals sensitive server configuration details including PHP version (7.0.30-0+deb9u1), installed extensions, environment variables, server paths, and system details (Linux Amazon Linux 2023 kernel). phpinfo.php is intended for debugging only and should never be accessible in production.
Remediation
Remove or restrict access to phpinfo.php. If required for debugging, restrict access by IP address via Apache configuration or remove the file entirely from production deployments.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange confirms a 200 OK response to an unauthenticated (security=low cookie) GET request for phpinfo.php, with the response body explicitly exposing PHP version 7.0.30 (EOL), full $_SERVER and $_ENV superglobals, server paths, and PDO MySQL configuration. This is a publicly accessible debug page on a production-facing EC2 instance leaking actionable reconnaissance data.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/phpinfo.php
MEDIUM Verbose Server Error Messages Reveal Database Type and Version WSTG-INFO-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/sqli/ 90%
Description
The application returns verbose database error messages that expose the underlying database technology. When a SQL injection probe is submitted to the /vulnerabilities/sqli/ endpoint, the response reveals that the database is MariaDB and includes the exact SQL syntax error. Additionally, 404 error pages expose the Apache version and operating system. This information aids attackers in crafting targeted exploits.
Remediation
Configure PHP to suppress database error messages in production (display_errors = Off, log_errors = On). Implement custom error pages that do not reveal server internals. Use PDO exceptions and catch them to display generic error messages.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange shows the application returning a raw MariaDB SQL syntax error message in response to a single-quote injection probe at /vulnerabilities/sqli/, confirming both SQL injection susceptibility and verbose error disclosure. The 404 page additionally leaks Apache/2.4.25 version and OS details, providing attackers with precise targeting information.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/sqli/
MEDIUM CloudWatch log metric filter and alarm exist for Network ACL (NACL) change events cloudwatch_changes_to_network_acls_alarm_configured prowler arn:aws:logs:us-east-2:760689105616:log-group 50%
Description
CloudTrail records for **Network ACL changes** are matched by a CloudWatch Logs metric filter with an associated alarm for events like `CreateNetworkAcl`, `CreateNetworkAclEntry`, `DeleteNetworkAcl`, `DeleteNetworkAclEntry`, `ReplaceNetworkAclEntry`, and `ReplaceNetworkAclAssociation`.
Remediation
Implement a CloudWatch Logs metric filter and alarm for NACL change events from CloudTrail and route alerts to responders. Enforce **least privilege** on NACL management, require **change control**, and use **defense in depth** with configuration monitoring and flow logs to validate and monitor network posture.
🤖 AI Triage: The finding title and description are ambiguous — phrasing like 'log metric filter and alarm exist' could indicate either a PASS (monitoring is in place) or a FAIL (it is missing). Without explicit pass/fail status in the evidence, this is a genuine CIS monitoring gap if the alarm is absent, but the description doesn't clearly confirm absence. The resource context (a log group ARN) exists but no filter or alarm config is provided to confirm the gap.
Resource

Description
**CloudTrail** logs in CloudWatch include a metric filter for **root account activity** (`{ $.userIdentity.type = "Root" && $.userIdentity.invokedBy NOT EXISTS && $.eventType != "AwsServiceEvent" }`) and a linked CloudWatch alarm that triggers when the filter matches.
Remediation
Enable real-time alerts for **root activity** using a log metric filter and a high-priority alarm with notifications. Reduce exposure: enforce **least privilege**, keep root for *break-glass* with MFA, disable root access keys, and route alerts into incident response for **defense in depth**.
🤖 AI Triage: Lack of a metric filter and alarm for root account usage (CIS 3.3) is a genuine and high-priority gap; root activity should always be alerted on since root has unrestricted access. The finding targets a specific log group ARN and no compensating monitoring control is evidenced.

MEDIUM CloudWatch log metric filter and alarm exist for Management Console sign-in without MFA cloudwatch_log_metric_filter_sign_in_without_mfa prowler arn:aws:logs:us-east-2:760689105616:log-group 60%
Description
**CloudTrail logs** in CloudWatch are assessed for a metric filter and alarm that detect console logins where `$.eventName = ConsoleLogin` and `$.additionalEventData.MFAUsed != \"Yes\"`. This reflects whether alerting exists for sign-ins that occur without **MFA**.
Remediation
Enforce **MFA** for all console-capable identities and maintain alerts for `ConsoleLogin` with `MFAUsed != \"Yes\"`. Apply **least privilege**, route alarms to monitored channels, and tune for SSO to reduce noise. Test alarms regularly and review coverage as part of **defense in depth**.
🤖 AI Triage: Absence of an alert for console logins without MFA (CIS 3.2) is a genuine security monitoring gap. Without this alert, credential-based attacks where MFA is bypassed or not enforced would be invisible. No compensating control such as an IdP-enforced MFA policy is referenced.

MEDIUM CloudWatch Logs metric filter and alarm exist for unauthorized API calls cloudwatch_log_metric_filter_unauthorized_api_calls prowler arn:aws:logs:us-east-2:760689105616:log-group 60%
Description
**CloudWatch Logs** for CloudTrail include a metric filter that matches unauthorized API errors (`$.errorCode="*UnauthorizedOperation"` or `$.errorCode="AccessDenied*"`) and a linked alarm that triggers when events match the filter.
Remediation
Enable real-time **alerting** by adding a CloudWatch Logs metric filter for unauthorized errors (`*UnauthorizedOperation`, `AccessDenied*`) and associating it with an alarm that notifies responders. - Enforce **least privilege** to reduce noise - Integrate with IR tooling for **defense in depth**
🤖 AI Triage: Missing metric filter and alarm for unauthorized API calls (CIS 3.1) means access-denial events indicating reconnaissance or privilege abuse would not trigger alerts. This is a standard CIS benchmark requirement and the gap is real without evidence of an alternative detection mechanism.
Resource

MEDIUM Network ACL does not allow ingress from the Internet to TCP port 22 (SSH) ec2_networkacl_allow_ingress_tcp_port_22 prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:network-acl/acl-0394c0b0123ced9aa 75%
Description
**VPC network ACLs** are evaluated for inbound rules that permit `0.0.0.0/0` to access **SSH** on `TCP 22` at the subnet boundary.
Remediation
Apply **least privilege** at the subnet layer: - Do not allow `0.0.0.0/0` to `TCP 22` - Restrict SSH to trusted sources, or avoid direct SSH via **Session Manager** or a bastion behind **VPN** Pair tight **security groups** with periodic rule reviews and change control to maintain **defense in depth**.
🤖 AI Triage: The NACL acl-0394c0b0123ced9aa in us-east-1 is flagged for allowing 0.0.0.0/0 inbound on TCP 22. NACLs are stateless subnet-boundary controls, so this is a real permissive rule; whether exploitation is possible depends on security group rules, but the NACL itself provides no SSH restriction from the internet.
Resource

AWS Account

**VPC network ACLs** are evaluated for inbound rules that permit `0.0.0.0/0` to access **SSH** on `TCP 22` at the subnet boundary.
Remediation
Apply **least privilege** at the subnet layer: - Do not allow `0.0.0.0/0` to `TCP 22` - Restrict SSH to trusted sources, or avoid direct SSH via **Session Manager** or a bastion behind **VPN** Pair tight **security groups** with periodic rule reviews and change control to maintain **defense in depth**.
🤖 AI Triage: NACL acl-0f4a0905d0267aff1 in us-east-2 also allows 0.0.0.0/0 on TCP 22. Same analysis as finding f86dc6a4; the subnet-level control is open and relies entirely on security groups for SSH restriction, reducing defense-in-depth.

MEDIUM Network ACL does not allow ingress from the Internet to TCP port 3389 (RDP) ec2_networkacl_allow_ingress_tcp_port_3389 prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:network-acl/acl-0394c0b0123ced9aa 75%
Description
**VPC network ACLs** with inbound rules allowing **RDP** on `TCP 3389` from `0.0.0.0/0` are identified. Assessment focuses on subnet-level ACL entries that permit this traffic.
Remediation
Enforce **least privilege**: do not allow `TCP 3389` from `0.0.0.0/0` in network ACLs. - Restrict RDP to specific admin IP ranges - Prefer **bastion hosts** or **Session Manager** over direct RDP - Use private subnets and layer controls for **defense in depth**

MEDIUM IAM password policy requires passwords to be at least 14 characters long iam_password_policy_minimum_length_14 prowler arn:aws:iam:us-east-2:760689105616:password-policy 65%
Description
**IAM password policy** is assessed for the **minimum password length** setting, confirming it meets `>= 14` characters for IAM console users.
Remediation
Set the **minimum password length** to `>= 14` (prefer `16+`). - Require mixed character types and prevent reuse - Enforce **MFA** for all console users - Prefer SSO over local IAM users - Apply least privilege and monitor authentication events
🤖 AI Triage: The IAM password policy does not meet the 14-character minimum requirement. This is a straightforward configuration check with no compensating control visible; if SSO/federated auth is used exclusively, IAM password policy becomes less relevant, but that context is absent here.
Resource
arn:aws:iam:us-east-2:760689105616:password-policy
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-east-2)
MEDIUM IAM password policy prevents reuse of the last 24 passwords iam_password_policy_reuse_24 prowler arn:aws:iam:us-east-2:760689105616:password-policy 65%
Description
**IAM account password policy** uses **password reuse prevention** set to `24` remembered passwords (maximum history) for IAM users
Remediation
Set the password policy to remember `24` previous passwords to block reuse. Combine with **MFA**, strong length and complexity, and avoid rotation practices that encourage predictable patterns. Apply **least privilege** and monitor authentication events as part of **defense in depth**.
🤖 AI Triage: The IAM password reuse prevention policy does not enforce 24-password history. Like the minimum-length finding, this is a direct policy misconfiguration; the absence of evidence for SSO-only access means local IAM users could reuse passwords.

MEDIUM VPC flow logs are enabled vpc_flow_logs_enabled prowler arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:vpc/vpc-03d1632e1404b5ec3 55%
Description
**AWS VPCs** have **Flow Logs** configured to capture IP traffic for their network interfaces and deliver records to a logging destination. VPCs lacking an active flow log configuration are highlighted.
Remediation
Enable **VPC Flow Logs** for all VPCs to provide baseline telemetry. Prefer capturing at least `REJECT` and, for sensitive networks, `ALL`. Send logs to a centralized, access-controlled destination with retention. Apply **least privilege** to writers/readers and integrate with monitoring for **defense in depth**.
🤖 AI Triage: The finding title says 'VPC flow logs are enabled' but the description and remediation context indicate this fires when flow logs are NOT configured. This is a compliance gap finding for vpc-03d1632e1404b5ec3 in us-east-1 with no evidence the flow logs are actually present, making it a real (if moderate-risk) logging gap rather than a false positive.
Resource
arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:760689105616:vpc/vpc-03d1632e1404b5ec3
AWS Account
760689105616 (us-east-1)

Send logs to a centralized, access-controlled destination with retention. Apply **least privilege** to writers/readers and integrate with monitoring for **defense in depth**.

MEDIUM Sensitive Configuration File Publicly Accessible (/config/config.inc.php) WSTG-CONF-09 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/config/config.inc.php 55%
Description
The application configuration file /config/config.inc.php is publicly readable over HTTP without authentication. The file contains commented-out AWS credential templates (AKIA key ID and secret access key placeholders). While the current values are placeholders ("YOUR_KEY_ID_HERE"), the file structure confirms this path is intended for storing real AWS credentials, and the file is not protected by Apache access controls or authentication. If real credentials were inserted (or were previously present and cached), they would be fully exposed. The file is served with Content-Type text/html and returns HTTP 200 regardless of authentication status.
Remediation
1. Immediately restrict access to /config/ directory via Apache .htaccess or main config: Deny from all / Require all denied. 2. Remove any real credentials from this file if they were ever present. 3. Move configuration files outside the webroot entirely (above the DocumentRoot). 4. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Parameter Store) for AWS credentials instead of flat files. 5. Audit git history to verify real credentials were never committed.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange confirms the file /config/config.inc.php is publicly accessible without authentication and returns HTTP 200 with commented-out AWS credential template lines. While the current values are placeholders and not real credentials, the path is unprotected and intended for credential storage per the 'TODO: remove before pushing' comments, representing a real misconfiguration that could expose credentials if ever populated.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/config/config.inc.php
MEDIUM DVWA Setup Page Exposes Default Credentials and Internal Paths WSTG-CONF-02 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/setup.php 90%
Description
The DVWA setup page at /setup.php is accessible without restriction (HTTP 200) and discloses: (1) Default administrator credentials ("admin" // "password"), (2) The full filesystem path to the config file (/var/www/html/config/config.inc.php), (3) Whether the config directory is writable by the web server (Yes - www-data), (4) The database password (masked as ******). Additionally, Apache's server-status page (/server-status) returns HTTP 403 with the server version in the error page footer ("Apache/2.4.25 (Debian) Server at... Port 80"). The server version Apache/2.4.25 is significantly outdated (released 2017) and has known vulnerabilities. No /server-info page was found (404).
Remediation
1. Restrict access to /setup.php via IP allowlist or authentication, or remove it from production. 2. Upgrade Apache from 2.4.25 to current stable version (2.4.62+) — version 2.4.25 has multiple known CVEs. 3. Configure Apache ServerTokens to 'Prod' to suppress version disclosure. 4. Ensure the config directory is not world-writable by the web server.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange confirms /setup.php is unauthenticated and explicitly discloses default admin credentials ('admin'/'password'), the full filesystem path (/var/www/html/config/config.inc.php), and that the config directory is writable by www-data. This is a directly exploitable configuration disclosure on what appears to be a production-accessible DVWA instance running an outdated Apache 2.4.25.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/setup.php
MEDIUM Outdated Apache Web Server Version Disclosed (Apache/2.4.25 Debian) WSTG-CONF-01 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/ 60%
Description
The server discloses its exact version in the Server response header on every HTTP response: "Apache/2.4.25 (Debian)". Apache 2.4.25 was released in December 2016 and has numerous known CVEs. Open ports per nmap: TCP/22 (SSH - OpenSSH 8.7), TCP/80 (HTTP - Apache 2.4.25). Ports 443, 3306, 8080, 8443, 21 are filtered. The SSH service (OpenSSH 8.7) is also accessible, representing an additional attack surface if weak credentials are in use. No reverse proxy or CDN headers detected (no Via, CF-RAY, X-Cache, etc.).
Remediation
1. Upgrade Apache to the current stable release (2.4.62+). 2. Set ServerTokens to 'Prod' in Apache config to suppress version info from headers. 3. Set ServerSignature to 'Off' to suppress version from error pages. 4. Apply all Debian security patches for the installed Apache version. 5. Consider restricting SSH access (port 22) to known IP ranges.
🤖 AI Triage: The Server header 'Apache/2.4.25 (Debian)' is confirmed on every response, exposing a version released in December 2016 with multiple known CVEs. Version disclosure alone is low-medium risk, but combined with the confirmed outdated version it represents a real attack surface reduction issue; however, exploitability depends on whether specific CVEs are unpatched.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/
MEDIUM Content Security Policy Missing or Critically Weak Across Application WSTG-CONF-12 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/ 50%
Description
No Content-Security-Policy header is present on any standard application page (/login.php, /index.php, /). The only CSP present is on /vulnerabilities/csp/ (an intentional DVWA challenge page), and that CSP is itself critically weak: it allows scripts from external domains including pastebin.com and the bare domain example.com (without HTTPS). The absence of CSP across the application means there is no browser-enforced mitigation against XSS attacks. Any reflected or stored XSS payload (which DVWA is intentionally vulnerable to) will execute without browser-level defense. This is especially significant given that FINDING-004 confirmed the session cookie lacks HttpOnly, making XSS-to-session-theft a complete attack chain.
Remediation
1. Implement a Content-Security-Policy header on all application pages, starting with a restrictive default: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'. 2. Remove external domains from script-src unless strictly necessary. 3. Never allow pastebin.com or other user-content-hosting domains in script-src. 4. Use CSP nonces or hashes instead of 'unsafe-inline' for any inline scripts required. 5. Test CSP coverage using a CSP evaluator tool before deployment.
🤖 AI Triage: The evidence confirms no CSP header on /login.php or /index.php, which is a real configuration gap. However, this is a DVWA instance that is intentionally vulnerable by design, so the absence of CSP is expected for this application; the practical risk is real but inherent to the application's purpose as a training tool.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/
MEDIUM Insecure Direct Object Reference — User Data Accessible by Arbitrary ID WSTG-ATHZ-04 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/sqli/ 40%
Description
The SQL Injection vulnerability module at /vulnerabilities/sqli/ exposes all application user records via an IDOR vulnerability. Any authenticated user can retrieve the first name and surname of any other user account by manipulating the "id" GET parameter. No authorization check verifies that the requesting user owns the requested record. All 5 user accounts (IDs 1-5) were enumerated successfully, including the admin account (ID=1). While this specific endpoint is the SQL Injection demonstration module in DVWA, the IDOR pattern demonstrates the absence of object-level authorization throughout the application. Users exposed: admin/admin (ID 1), Gordon Brown (ID 2), Hack Me (ID 3), Pablo Picasso (ID 4), Bob Smith (ID 5).
Remediation
Implement object-level authorization checks: verify that the authenticated user has permission to access the requested record before returning data. For user-specific data, confirm that the session user ID matches the requested object ID (or the user has an admin role). Use parameterized queries and enforce row-level access controls at the database or application layer.
🤖 AI Triage: The HTTP exchange confirms unauthenticated-by-role access to other users' data via the id parameter, but this endpoint is explicitly the DVWA SQL Injection demonstration module — intentionally vulnerable by design. The IDOR pattern is real but the endpoint exists specifically to demonstrate these vulnerabilities, making this a low-confidence finding as a genuine production security issue.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/vulnerabilities/sqli/
MEDIUM No Account Lockout Mechanism on Login Form WSTG-ATHN-03 autopentest http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php 70%
Description
The DVWA login form does not implement any account lockout, rate limiting, or brute-force protection. Ten consecutive failed login attempts for the admin account all returned the same generic "Login failed" response with HTTP 302 redirect to login.php. No CAPTCHA was presented, no lockout occurred, and no delay was introduced between attempts. This enables unlimited brute-force attacks against any account.
Remediation
Implement account lockout after 5 failed attempts (temporary lockout of 15-30 minutes). Add progressive delays between attempts. Consider implementing CAPTCHA after 3 failed attempts. Log and alert on repeated failed login attempts from same IP. Implement IP-based rate limiting.
🤖 AI Triage: Ten consecutive failed login attempts all returned HTTP 302 to login.php with no lockout, CAPTCHA, or delay, as confirmed by the reproduction steps. No account lockout on a login form with known default credentials ('admin'/'password') creates a directly exploitable brute-force path, though the overall risk is contextual to whether this instance is exposed to untrusted users.
Resource
http://ec2-18-188-101-84.us-east-2.compute.amazonaws.com/login.php
Remediation Playbook
Quick Wins (8 actions)
✓ Breaks Chain exposed-config-to-db-compromise
Remove /config/config.inc.php and config.inc.php.bak from the webroot and block .bak/.inc via web server rules.
✓ Breaks Chain setup-reinit-to-admin-takeover
Require authentication on setup.php or remove it from production.
✓ Breaks Chain security-cookie-bypass-to-exploit-chain
Enforce IMDSv2 on the DVWA EC2 instance (i-05fb02cc8e210b567) to block SSRF-based credential theft.
✓ Breaks Chain xss-csrf-password-takeover
Add CSRF tokens and require the current password on the password-change endpoint.
exposed-config-to-db-compromise
Rotate the leaked database credentials immediately.
setup-reinit-to-admin-takeover
Change all default DVWA credentials to strong unique values.
security-cookie-bypass-to-exploit-chain
Validate the security level server-side instead of trusting the client cookie.
xss-csrf-password-takeover
Set HttpOnly, Secure and SameSite=Lax on session cookies.
Medium Effort (1 action)
exposed-config-to-db-compromise
Enforce HTTPS with redirect and HSTS.
